MATLAB Verify

Do you trust your chatbot to do math?

MATLAB Verify checks highlighted AI answers against MATLAB-backed verification before a student, analyst, or engineer accepts the result.

The goal is not to replace the chatbot. The goal is to give users a fast trust layer for math-heavy answers: highlight a claim, run a MATLAB-backed check, and see whether the answer is verified, refuted, or needs more context.

Highlight Verify See proof or counterexample

A browser extension sends the selected claim to a local helper, MATLAB verifies it, and the result returns in the workflow.

The Everyday Problem

A simple answer can still be wrong.

A chatbot can confidently add, differentiate, solve, convert units, or compute finance claims. The hard part for the user is not getting an answer. It is knowing whether that answer is safe to use.

That matters most in workflows where small mistakes are expensive: homework feedback, engineering notes, financial calculations, controls analysis, lab reports, and model documentation.

Verified Example

Homework-style arithmetic

A family buys 3 notebooks for $2.49 each, 2 pens for $1.25 each, and a backpack for $18.99.

3*2.49 + 2*1.25 + 18.99 = 28.96

The claim is basic on purpose. If users can verify simple arithmetic in the same place they ask the question, the same workflow can extend to harder symbolic and technical claims.

21% of people say AI-generated information can be trusted most or almost all of the time.
53% of surveyed students were concerned about inaccurate AI-generated information.
9% AI answer inaccuracy at massive scale still creates millions of bad answers.
Reliability is the wedge. People already use AI systems for learning and analysis, but they do not fully trust the output. MATLAB Verify turns the trust question into a visible workflow step instead of leaving it as a hidden judgment call.

What It Shows

Verification shows up where the work happens.

The demo is intentionally browser-native. Users do not have to copy an answer into MATLAB, write a script, or decide which function to call. The extension reads the selected claim, the helper normalizes it, and MATLAB returns a verdict that appears directly next to the AI response.

This makes the product feel like a math and engineering equivalent of citation-checking tools: it does not generate the answer, it checks the claim.

Verified

Everyday arithmetic

A receipt-style calculation is checked as a symbolic equality, then confirmed directly in the chat UI.

Not Verified

Derivative mistakes

The verifier catches a wrong derivative and reports that the claimed expression does not match MATLAB's symbolic result.

Not Verified

Finance calculations

Loan balance and cashflow functions can be checked against MATLAB-computed values.

Verified

Equation solutions

Claimed roots are verified against the extracted equation, including complex-valued solutions.

Current Coverage

Built for more than calculator checks.

The verifier routes claims to the right MATLAB-backed capability instead of treating every answer as plain text. That matters because a derivative claim, a loan balance, a unit conversion, and a control-stability statement need different verification logic.

Symbolic equivalenceChecks identities and simplified expressions.
CalculatorVerifies arithmetic and numeric equality claims.
DerivativesCompares claimed derivatives to MATLAB symbolic results.
Units consistencyChecks unit-bearing equalities and conversions.
LaplaceVerifies Laplace and inverse Laplace transform claims.
StatisticsChecks scalar statistics such as mean, variance, correlation, and tests.
Integral claimsVerifies definite integral results.
Limit claimsChecks symbolic limit claims, including one-sided limits.
Control stabilityVerifies basic stability claims for control systems.
FinanceChecks loan balance, NPV, IRR, payment, and cashflow claims.

How It Works

A local verification loop, not another chatbot guess.

The assistant can explain. MATLAB verifies. The architecture keeps the interaction lightweight for the user while preserving a real computation engine behind the verdict.

1

Extension

Captures highlighted text and surrounding context from the AI chat page.

2

Python helper

Normalizes copied math, validates claim shape, and chooses the best verifier.

3

MATLAB

Runs symbolic, numeric, finance, control, units, or statistics verification and returns JSON.

4

Result

Shows a compact verdict, normalized claim, and engine details in the workflow.

The important product behavior is routing. Instead of asking the user to know which MATLAB function proves a claim, the helper decides whether the highlighted text is symbolic, derivative, units, finance, control, statistics, integral, limit, or arithmetic.

Audience

People with MATLAB access who still live in AI chat.

The clearest wedge is students: they have math-heavy work, frequent AI usage, and a strong need to know when an answer is trustworthy. They may have access to MATLAB through a university license, but their first stop for help is increasingly an AI chat interface.

That creates an adoption opportunity. MATLAB can become the verification engine behind the AI workflow, even when the user is not explicitly starting in MATLAB.

  • Students: checking homework-style arithmetic, calculus, equations, units, and finance examples before submitting or studying from an AI answer.
  • Faculty and teaching staff: reviewing AI-assisted work, showing students how to validate answers, and reinforcing computational thinking.
  • Engineers and analysts: validating claims before copying them into reports, models, spreadsheets, notebooks, or code.
  • MathWorks adoption angle: drive MATLAB usage inside the AI workflows users already prefer, while reminding them that MATLAB is the trusted computational layer.

Why This Could Matter

Claim verification is becoming its own workflow category.

The closest analogs are research-verification tools: products that sit inside the user's workflow and make AI output more trustworthy. They do not ask users to stop using AI. They add a trust layer around it.

MATLAB Verify applies that pattern to technical and mathematical claims. The market signal is that users will pay for tools that reduce uncertainty inside high-frequency knowledge workflows.

Scite signal

A close workflow analog: verify claims inside the research process. It has reported 2M users, active subscribers, subscription revenue, a browser extension, and integrations with AI tools.

Consensus signal

Shows broad education and research demand. It grew from hundreds of thousands of monthly active users to millions of students, researchers, and faculty across universities.

Elicit signal

Shows the higher-end workflow version: researchers pay for reliability, structure, and workflow depth when AI output affects important decisions.

The parallel is not that MATLAB Verify is a research-citation tool. The parallel is the workflow pattern: users want AI assistance, but they also want evidence. For technical claims, MATLAB can provide that evidence.

Don't trust blindly. Verify in the workflow.

MATLAB Verify turns AI math answers into checkable claims, then brings the result back to the same place the user is already working. For students, it makes AI help safer. For instructors, it creates a teachable verification habit. For MathWorks, it puts MATLAB into the growing AI-assisted workflow instead of waiting for users to come back to a standalone app.
